original: To start, you can press on the Edit button (
The buttons on the side of the spreadsheet are your Row buttons. Pressing them will highlight the entire row. You can drag the corner of each row button to adjust the row’s height.
Similarly, buttons on the top of the spreadsheet are your Column buttons. Pressing them will highlight the column. You can drag the corner of each column button to change the column’s width.
Now, this is the spreadsheet’s Cell. Pressing on each cell will give you editing options for the cell.
When a cell is selected, this area will display the cell location. Use the Input area to type in what text you want to show in the cell.
This area is the text formatting area. You can toggle each button to change how you want your text to look in the selected cell. Options include: Bold, Italic, Underline and Strikethrough.
This button is the Toggle Text Align button. Press this button to change between left, center or right alignment of the text within the selected cell.
This is the Toggle Fill button. Press this button to change the shade of the selected cell’s background.
If you want to add some bordering around your cell, you can use these Toggle Border buttons to add a border line to the bottom, left, top or right of the select cell.
Is the screen too bright for
Finally, in this area, you can find options to Clear (
This concludes the overview for the Spreadsheet application.\nI hope you’ve found the information useful.\nUntil next time!

Oh yeah, that’s like the name of the game!
Change the current control scheme below.
To begin with, the title bar has a Pin button (
When the pin is enabled, you can press and hold the title bar to activate its move feature, and then you can drag to reposition the window anywhere you’d like on the screen.
To reset the location of the window, you can Close (
Next, this is the calculator’s Precision option (
If you want to change the look of the output, you can use this button to Toggle Digital Style (
In addition to that, this area is the calculator’s visual customization section. This is mainly for accessibility, but it can also add a little pizzazz to your office space.
You can choose to use the Toggle Color button (
And this button is to access the Opacity (
Now that I have your undivided attention, it’s time we move on to the main section of the calculator. I will explain what each part of the calculator is and how to properly use it.

Firstly, this is the Display viewport, showing your input history and the results of your calculations. There are also indicators on the far left side to signify if the resulting value is a negative value or if you have a saved value stored into memory.
This is your standard 10-digit Numpad that allows you to type numbers from 0 to 9, as well as a Decimal point (.).
If you make a mistake in your current entry, you can use the Backspace key (⌫) to erase the last character input.

Alternatively, you can use the Clear Entry button (CE) to clear the current entry. Pressing it again, or after making an evaluation, will Clear All (C), clearing the entry as well as the entire input history, and any displayed error messages.
As you know, these fancy looking buttons are a few of the commonly used mathematical constants. Use them if you don’t want to type out the values by hand.

Here, you’ll find the arithmetical Operator keys: Add (+), Subtract (−), Multiply (×), Divide (÷). To use them, type your first number, then the operation, and then your second number.\nYou can press the operation button again to repeatedly append the operation with the displayed value.

You will then want to press the Equals button (=) to evaluate your statement.\nKeep in mind that the operations are simple and will be evaluated in the order in which they were inputted.\nYou can press the button again if you want to repeat the last operation displayed in the input history.

Next up are the special operation buttons that will convert the value of currently displayed entry. You can inverse the value with the Negative button (±), take the entry’s radical value with the Square Root button (√), or convert the value to a percentage value with the Percent button (%).

And these are the Memory keys. They are used to alter a value stored in memory, which starts off as 0 by default.\nType your value, then press the M+ or M− buttons to add the value to or subtract the value from the memory value, respectively. If the memory value is a non-zero value, there will be an indicator signifying that on the display.
Use the Memory Recall button (MRC) to apply the current memory value.\nPress the button again (ClrM) to clear the memory value and reset it to 0.
Oh! I almost forgot.\nThis button over here is the Rotate button (
Anyway, that concludes the overview for the Calculator application.\nI hope you’ve found the information useful.\nUntil next time!

To begin with, the title bar has a Pin button (
After enabling the pin, you can activate its move feature by pressing and holding the title bar, then you will be able to drag it to reposition the window anywhere you’d like on the screen.
To reset the location of the window, you can Close (
On the title bar, you can press on the Edit button (
My my, how time flies! This is section is occupied by the Clock.
Next, this area is the calendar’s visual customization section. Feel free to toggle between options, especially if you have a hard time reading the interface with the current settings.
If you want to change the look of the clock, you can use this button to Toggle Digital Style (
When the clock is set to digital mode, you can use the Toggle Dark Mode button (
And for the main event, this is the Calendar section! You can use it to browse dates as well as save notes to any day you choose.
Oh look, it’s today!\n\nSpeaking of, if you want to add a note to a day...
...simply select the day from the calendar and fill in the Text Area. Then click on Create Note button. You can also chose to Delete an existing note or View the current notes list.
Finally, in this area, you can choose to Clear (
This about wraps it up for the Calendar application.\nThis should definitely help you stay organized!
Today I learned about the Calendar!
